channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: When Britney Spears’s Conservatorship Ended, Concern for Her Did Not
item.description: The pop star’s arrest on suspicion of driving under the influence this week was a breaking point, years after she regained control of her life and finances.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: Some observers have questioned whether Britney Spears, after successfully fighting to be released from a 13-year conservatorship, now needs more supervision.

item.title: Alysa Liu on Dancing Her Way to Olympic Gold: ‘The Music Carries My Body’
item.description: The free-spirited figure skater spoke to our dance critic about her approach to performing, the role of music and more.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas

item.title: Top National Symphony Leader Quits in New Blow to Kennedy Center
item.description: The executive director, Jean Davidson, said her departure reflects frustration at the turmoil that has engulfed the arts center.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s no secret that this has been a really hard year,” the executive director of the National Symphony Orchestra, Jean Davidson, said of her decision to leave her post after less than three years.

item.title: Timothée Chalamet Has a Point About Ballet
item.description: This art form is alive, and Chalamet, who comes from a dance family, knows it. But what value does ballet have for the world at large?
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas
item.media:description: Timothée Chalamet at a Knicks game in 2019. He said this week, “I don’t want to be working in ballet or opera, or, you know, things where it’s like, ‘Hey, keep this thing alive.’”

item.title: Boston Symphony Abruptly Ends Andris Nelsons’ Contract
item.description: The orchestra’s leadership announced on Friday that it and the conductor Andris Nelsons “were not aligned on future vision.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney

item.title: For the Friars, a Sad Sale of Their Club’s Last Vestiges
item.description: Friars Club memorabilia, including photos of Billy Crystal and Jack Benny’s violin, sold well at an auction that upset former members of the defunct showbiz fraternity.
item.dc:creator: Tom Mashberg

item.title: Inside Harry Styles’s ‘Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.’ With Kid Harpoon
item.description: The producer and songwriter has been responsible for some of this century’s biggest hits. His greatest skill may be remaining curious.
item.dc:creator: Grayson Haver Currin
item.category: Kiss All the Time, Disco Occasionally. (Album)
item.media:credit: Adali Schell for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tom Hull, a.k.a. Kid Harpoon, at home in California. He dragged a bunch of modular synths to recording sessions with Harry Styles for the pop star’s new album.

item.title: Bob Power on His Work on A Tribe Called Quest’s ‘The Low End Theory’
item.description: A Tribe Called Quest had the vision for “The Low End Theory.” The engineer Bob Power helped piece it together.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Cindy Ord/WireImage for The Recording Academy, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bob Power’s work on A Tribe Called Quest’s “The Low End Theory” has been lauded by listeners and critics since its release 35 years ago.

item.title: Chris Fleming: Is This the Funniest Special of the Year So Far?
item.description: Chris Fleming’s wild way with language is both dazzling and hilarious in “Live at the Palace,” an hour that veers in surprising directions.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.category: Chris Fleming: Live at the Palace (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Fleming’s new hour smartly strips away the set decoration to focus on the jokes.

item.title: Three 6 Mafia on Their Oscars Win, 20 Years Later
item.description: Twenty years ago, Three 6 Mafia’s Oscars triumph for “It’s Hard Out Here for a Pimp” helped an uptight awards show loosen up.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: The members of Three 6 Mafia celebrating their win onstage at the Oscars in 2006.

item.title: A Fast-Rising American Director Is Wowing the West End
item.description: After a prizewinning “Fiddler on the Roof” and a lauded take on Sondheim, Jordan Fein is tackling Arthur Miller’s enigmatic “Broken Glass.”
item.dc:creator: Andrew Dickson
item.media:credit: Ellie Smith for The New York Times
item.media:description: The American director Jordan Fein in London, where he has lived since 2019.

item.title: Batman and Daredevil Artist Klaus Janson Flies Solo in Gallery Show
item.description: Klaus Janson, known for his work on Daredevil and Batman, said he hoped his new solo show could give people “a growing appreciation of what comics can do.”
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines
item.media:credit: Janice Chung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The comic book artist Klaus Janson, who for years worked alongside Frank Miller, at his show at the Philippe Labaune Gallery in Chelsea.

item.title: A Philharmonic Conductor’s Concerts Surprise, for Better and Worse
item.description: Mirga Grazinyte-Tyla returned to the New York Philharmonic for a pair of programs, but only one formed a cohesive arc of ideas.
item.dc:creator: Corinna da Fonseca-Wollheim

item.title: A Film That Makes a Strong Argument for the Value of Debate
item.description: “Immutable” is about young debaters in a league in Washington, D.C., as well as about the skill itself in a world where yelling can seem the norm.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Alan Trustman, Who Wrote ‘Bullitt’ and ‘Thomas Crown Affair,’ Dies at 95
item.description: In a wide-ranging career, he was a Boston lawyer, a Hollywood screenwriter and a Swiss currency trader.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: Jeremy Larner, Who Wrote ‘The Candidate,’ a Political Film Classic, Dies at 88
item.description: His Oscar-winning 1972 screenplay starred Robert Redford as an idealistic public interest lawyer making a run for the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: From 1999: Charlotte Perriand, Designer, Is Dead at 96
item.description: Le Corbusier famously told her, “We don’t embroider cushions here,” when she sought a job at his studio. Then he recognized her talent for design.
item.dc:creator: Julie V. Iovine

item.title: From 2001: Aaliyah, 22, Singer Who First Hit the Charts at 14
item.description: She was seen as a hip-hop temptress when she was still a teenager, and her albums “Age Ain’t Nothing but a Number” and “One in a Million” sold millions of copies.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: Aaliyah in 1995.

item.title: From 2014: Maya Angelou, Lyrical Witness of the Jim Crow South, Dies at 86
item.description: Her landmark book “I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings” was among the first 20th-century autobiographies of a Black woman to reach a wide readership.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox

item.title: From 1983: Karen Carpenter, 32, Is Dead; Singer Teamed With Brother
item.description: The Carpenters sold more than 30 million records with the irresistible combination of her soft-rock contralto and her brother’s lush arrangements.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Karen Carpenter performing around 1975.

item.title: From 2007: Anna Nicole Smith Dies at 39
item.description: A former Playboy centerfold, actress and TV personality, she was also known for being rich (sporadically) and litigious (chronically).
item.dc:creator: Abby Goodnough and Margalit Fox
item.media:description: Anna Nicole Smith in 1993.

item.title: From 1995: Grammy-Winning Singer Selena Killed in Shooting at Texas Motel
item.description: Known as the queen of Tejano music, she was beloved as an idol and a heartthrob on both sides of the Mexican border.
item.dc:creator: Sam Howe Verhovek
item.media:credit: Dave Einsel/Houston Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Selena performing in Houston in 1993.

item.title: From 1962: Brilliant Stardom and Personal Tragedy Punctuated the Life of Marilyn Monroe
item.description: One of the most famous stars in Hollywood, she suffered severe setbacks in the last years of her life.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: From 1981: Death Ends a 39-Year Career, Starting With Child Roles at 4
item.description: Natalie Wood evolved from a child star into a teenage ingénue and then a mature actress, until her trajectory was tragically cut short.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:description: Natalie Wood around 1969.

item.title: From 1992: Marlene Dietrich, 90, Symbol of Glamour, Dies
item.description: “Dietrich is something that never existed before and may never exist again,” the actor Maurice Chevalier said of her. “That’s a woman.”
item.dc:creator: Peter B. Flint
item.media:credit: Eugene Robert Richee/John Kobal Foundation, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Marlene Dietrich in 1935. She was an international symbol of glamour and sex for more than half a century.

item.title: From 2019: Toni Morrison, Towering Novelist of the Black Experience, Dies at 88
item.description: Ms. Morrison, who wrote “Beloved” and “Song of Solomon,” was the first African-American woman to win the Nobel in literature.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:description: Toni Morrison in 2008.

item.title: From 1969: Judy Garland, 47, Found Dead
item.description: She dazzled audiences in “The Wizard of Oz” and “A Star Is Born,” but her successes were later overshadowed by addiction and other struggles.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Judy Garland around 1941.

item.title: From 1977: Maria Callas, 53, Is Dead of Heart Attack in Paris
item.description: Considered the most exciting opera singer of her time, she thrilled audiences with her penchant for spectacle onstage and in her personal life.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Ericson
item.media:description: Maria Callas in the 2017 documentary “Maria By Callas,” directed by Tom Volf.

item.title: From 2005: Ruth Clement Bond, 101, Quilter and Civic Leader, Is Dead
item.description: She helped transform the American quilt from a utilitarian bed covering into a work of avant-garde social commentary.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox

item.title: From 2010: Lucille Clifton, Poet Who Explored Black Lives, Dies at 73
item.description: A distinguished American poet, she examined the experience of being Black and female in the 20th century.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:credit: Afro American Newspapers/Gado, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Lucille Clifton in 1995.

item.title: From 1967: Dorothy Parker, Literary Wit, Dies at Age 73
item.description: She enjoyed a lifelong reputation as a glittering, annihilating humorist. For her epitaph, she suggested, “Excuse My Dust.”
item.dc:creator: Alden Whitman
item.media:description: Dorothy Parker in 1941. Her writing often appeared in The New Yorker, starting with the magazine’s second issue, on Feb. 28, 1925.

item.title: From 1992: Audre Lorde, 58, a Poet, Memoirist and Lecturer, Dies
item.description: Her large body of work, which included poetry, essays and autobiography, reflected her hatred of racial and sexual prejudice.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Audre Lorde in 1983. Six years later, her collection of essays, “A Burst of Light,” won an American Book Award.

item.title: From 1959: Billie Holiday Dies Here at 44; Jazz Singer Had Wide Influence
item.description: Miss Holiday, who became a singer more out of desperation than desire, was one of the most influential jazz musicians of her time.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Billie Holiday performing in Manhattan in 1947.

item.title: From 2011: Amy Winehouse, British Soul Singer With a Troubled Life, Dies at 27
item.description: A British singer who found worldwide fame with her sassy, hip-hop-inflected take on retro soul, she became a tabloid fixture because of addiction problems.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Amy Winehouse sold millions of albums and won five Grammy Awards.

item.title: From 2019: Agnès Varda, Influential French New Wave Filmmaker, Dies at 90
item.description: She was closely associated with the film movement known as the New Wave, although her reimagining of cinematic conventions predated it.
item.dc:creator: John Anderson

item.title: From 2000: Hedy Lamarr, Sultry Star Who Reigned in Hollywood
item.description: A temptress on the silver screen in the 1930s and ’40s, she later became an inventor.
item.dc:creator: Richard Severo
item.media:credit: Clarence Sinclair Bull/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hedy Lamarr posing for a promotional portrait for the 1944 film “The Heavenly Body.”

item.title: From 1960: Zora Hurston, 57, Writer, Is Dead
item.description: Although her books, written in the dialect of the Deep South, established her as one of the foremost writers of Black folklore, she died in obscurity.
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press
item.media:description: Zora Neale Hurston in the late 1930s or early ’40s. Her eight books, and the numerous short stories and articles she published in magazines, were well received by critics.

item.title: From 2018: Aretha Franklin, Indomitable ‘Queen of Soul,’ Dies at 76
item.description: With hits like “Respect” and “Chain of Fools,” she defined a female archetype: sensual and strong, long-suffering but ultimately indomitable.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Don Hogan Charles/The New York Times
item.media:description: Aretha Franklin in 1968, performing at the Democratic National Convention in Chicago.

item.title: From 1975: Josephine Baker Is Dead in Paris at 68
item.description: She performed with a string of bananas tied around her waist, an electrifying act that led her to become first a local sensation in Paris, and then an international star.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times

item.title: From 2006: Oriana Fallaci, Incisive Italian Journalist, Is Dead at 77
item.description: An iconoclastic journalist, she was known for her war coverage and her aggressive, revealing interviews with the powerful.
item.dc:creator: Ian Fisher
item.media:description: Oriana Fallaci in the 1980s. A glamorous figure with an ever-present cigarette, she was once described in The Los Angeles Times as “the journalist to whom virtually no world figure would say no.”

item.title: From 1961: Anna May Wong Is Dead at 54; Actress Won Movie Fame in ’24
item.description: With a film career spanning three decades, Miss Wong, who rose to stardom with “The Thief of Baghdad,” was acclaimed as a versatile and talented performer.
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press
item.media:credit: Daily Herald Archive/National Science & Media Museum/SSPL, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Miss Wong in 1934. She made her first trip to China two years later.

item.title: From 1975: Hannah Arendt, Political Scientist, Is Dead at 69
item.description: She caused controversy with books like “Eichmann in Jerusalem,” published in 1963, which grew out of her coverage of Adolf Eichmann’s trial for The New Yorker.
item.dc:creator: David Bird
item.media:credit: Fred Stein Archive/Archive Photos, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hannah Arendt in 1944. She fled to the United States four years earlier, when the Nazis were pressing in on France.

item.title: From 2003: Leni Riefenstahl, Filmmaker and Nazi Propagandist, Dies at 101
item.description: Her documentaries earned her acclaim as a cinematic genius, as well as criticism for putting her talent at the service of Hitler.
item.dc:creator: Alan Riding
item.media:credit: DB Walter Frentz Archiv/picture-alliance/dpa, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Leni Riefenstahl in 1936, the year that the German Olympic Committee commissioned her to record the Berlin Olympics.

item.title: Five International Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include a Mexican drama, a Japanese crime thriller, an absurdist French comedy and more.
item.dc:creator: Devika Girish
item.media:description: Luisa Huertas in “We Shall Not Be Moved.”

item.title: 8 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘War Machine’ Review: On the Fritz
item.description: The “Reacher” star Alan Ritchson can’t troubleshoot this alien invasion sci-fi flick.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: Late Night Isn’t Sad to See Kristi Noem Leave Homeland Security
item.description: “Sounds like someone’s about to become the FIFA secretary of homeland security,” Stephen Colbert joked.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2, Episode 9 Recap: Family Separation
item.description: A young boy arrives at the emergency room this week with a firework-related injury, but his troubles run much deeper than that.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: From left, Sasha Compere, Noah Wyle and Isa Briones in “The Pitt.” Episode 9 included a story line in which a patient’s parents were seized during an immigration hearing and deported to Haiti.

item.title: Wallace Shawn’s ‘What We Did Before Our Moth Days’ Is Purgatory Done Right
item.description: The playwright and his collaborator André Gregory are together again, delivering a sumptuous set of interlinked monologues about life, death and betrayal.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)
item.media:description: From left, Hope Davis, John Early, Josh Hamilton and Maria Dizzia in “What We Did Before Our Moth Days” at the Greenwich House Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in March
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Seph Rodney covers Deborah Roberts’s collages, Ursula von Rydingsvard’s wood outcroppings and Noel W Anderson’s superstars.
item.dc:creator: Seph Rodney

item.title: António Lobo Antunes, One of Europe’s Most Revered Writers, Dies at 83
item.description: In a career studded with literary awards, he was the author of dozens of books that grappled with his nation’s legacy of dictatorship and colonialism.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: António Lobo Antunes in 1988. The literary critic George Steiner called him “a novelist of the very first rank” and likened him to Joseph Conrad and William Faulkner.

item.title: Pokémon Company Objects to White House’s Political Memes
item.description: “No permission was granted for the use of our intellectual property,” the company said. The Trump administration frequently promotes policies with content from video games.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The White House used an image from the new game Pokémon Pokopia in a social media post saying “Make America Great Again.”

item.title: Megumi Yuasa’s Six-Decade-Long Love Affair With Clay
item.description: At 87, the sculptor and ceramist Megumi Yuasa is having his first solo exhibition in the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Rose Courteau and Luisa Dörr

item.title: Paris Again Merges as a Mecca for the Arts
item.description: Many of the galleries showing at TEFAF Maastricht are from France, a sign of the country’s growing presence in fairs and overall rising influence in art and collecting.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We are still very open in Paris,” said Nicolas Fournery, a gallerist who is showing at TEFAF for the second year. “When you compare with the United States and other countries, they are closing, but we are not in the same mood in France.”

item.title: This Small Statue in Brussels Has a Large Bladder and Lots of Outfits
item.description: Manneken Pis, the famed bronze statue of a little boy who always has to go, has an official dresser in charge of his more than 1,190 outfits.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)

item.title: How the Florists Behind TEFAF Maastricht Work Their Magic
item.description: A Dutch florist has crafted the fair’s massive, colorful arrangements since 1988. Their secrets? Early mornings, intense planning and thousands of flowers.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Morelli

item.title: Welcome to Maastricht, Where All Things French Flourish
item.description: Evident is the vast influence that France has had upon the Netherlands city, the result of its geography, history and the population’s affinity for its culture.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Sciolino and Ilvy Njiokiktjien
item.media:description: The City Hall tower in Maastricht, the southernmost city in the Netherlands.

item.title: Margrethe Odgaard’s Explorations of Color Come to TEFAF
item.description: Margrethe Odgaard, whose new textiles will be featured at TEFAF Maastricht, aims to define the distinctive hues preferred in various places, by different cultures.
item.dc:creator: Alec Scott
item.media:credit: Carsten Snejbjerg for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Danish designer and textile artist Margrethe Odgaard, who has helped create colors for many brands and products, is now using hand-dyed fabrics to create art pieces.

item.title: TEFAF Maastricht, Known for Old Art, Faces the Future
item.description: The Dutch art fair, renowned for art and antiquities, works to prove its relevance as the wider world changes around it.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn

item.title: At TEFAF Maastricht, a Paris Gallery Honors a Revolutionary French Painter
item.description: In the ’70s, Patrick Saytour and his fellow avant-gardists took their work outside the frame, exploding the notion of what painting might be.
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa
